title: Lastreopsis davalliiformis
taxonomy: Lastreopsis davalliiformis is classified within the family Dryopteridaceae, which is a group of ferns commonly known as the wood ferns or the autumn ferns. This species belongs to the genus Lastreopsis, which is native to Australia and New Zealand. The family Dryopteridaceae is a part of the larger division Pteridophyta, which includes all the ferns. The classification of Lastreopsis davalliiformis is further divided into the kingdom Plantae, the division Pteridophyta, the class Polypodiopsida, the order Polypodiales, the family Dryopteridaceae, the genus Lastreopsis, and the species L. davalliiformis. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics, such as its leaf shape, growth habit, and reproductive structures.

intro: Lastreopsis davalliiformis, also known as Davallia, is a type of fern that belongs to the family Dryopteridaceae. This plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may aid in reducing pain and inflammation, as well as supporting immune function and overall well-being. The bioactive constituents of Lastreopsis davalliiformis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and triterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Lastreopsis davalliiformis is often prepared as a tea, infusion, or tincture, and is used to treat various health conditions, including fever, colds, and respiratory issues.
